abstract:
Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) represent the leading non-viral platform for gene delivery, yet the specific influence of their physicochemical properties on performance following intramuscular (IM) injection remains poorly understood. In this study, we engineered a series of LNPs with varying sizes, surface charges, and PEGylated lipid compositions to evaluate their transfection efficiency and biodistribution using luciferase mRNA (mLuc) as a reporter.

Our findings demonstrate that smaller LNPs and those with a neutral surface charge achieve significantly higher transfection levels after IM administration. Interestingly, while these factors dictated protein expression, they did not substantially alter the overall anatomical distribution of the particles themselves. We further observed that PEGylated lipids with shorter acyl chains (C14 vs. C18) markedly enhanced transfection by facilitating more efficient cellular uptake and endosomal escape. Detailed mechanistic analysis revealed that the choice of PEGylated lipid dictates the specific endocytic pathway—clathrin-mediated vs. caveolae-mediated—primarily due to differences in the rate of PEG shedding.

By systematic evaluation of these parameters, this work identifies the critical role of nanoparticle architecture and PEG lipid kinetics in optimizing the intramuscular route. These insights resolve key knowledge gaps and provide specific design principles for maximizing the efficacy of mRNA-based vaccines and therapeutics.
